title: Four-shaft M's and O's
subtitle: Weaving the American colonial alternating block structure in cotton or linen
excerpt: M's and O's is a traditional American colonial block weave that produces a distinctive textured surface of alternating warp and weft floats in a geometric grid. This entry covers the threading, tie-up, and treadling for a standard four-shaft M's and O's sample.
sourceNotes: M's and O's documented in Mary Atwater, The Shuttle-Craft Book of American Hand-Weaving (1928, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in general Coverlet and colonial weaving documentation. The structure was widely used in 18th and 19th-century American household weaving.

glossaryTerms.definition: The pattern by which each warp thread is assigned to a specific shaft. In M's and O's, the threading follows a simple repeating unit using all four shafts.
A thread that passes over two or more opposing threads without interlacing. In M's and O's, alternating blocks of warp floats and weft floats create the grid-like texture.
The number of warp threads per unit of width. M's and O's works at a slightly more open sett than plain weave to allow the float blocks to show clearly.

content.text: M's and O's is named for the visual alternation of blocks that resemble the letters M and O when viewed from the right side of the cloth. The structure uses all four shafts to produce two independent block sequences: one block shows warp
where the other shows weft floats, creating a visual contrast without colour changes. The
uses a simple four-thread unit that is straightforward to set up; the
is only slightly more open than plain weave.

items.name: Cotton or linen warp yarn (8/2 or 10/2)

content.text: Thread on a straight draw: 1-2-3-4, repeated across the full warp width. Thread at a sett slightly more open than plain weave for the yarn: if 8/2 cotton plain weaves at 24 epi, set M's and O's at 18 to 20 epi.

Tie: treadle 1 to shafts 1 and 2, treadle 2 to shafts 3 and 4, treadle 3 to shafts 1 and 4, treadle 4 to shafts 2 and 3. Treadle sequence for the full pattern: 1, 2, 1, 2, 3, 4, 3, 4 (repeat). Treadles 1 and 2 produce blocks of warp floats; treadles 3 and 4 produce blocks of weft floats. Each group of four picks forms one complete M-and-O unit.

Remove the cloth from the loom and secure the ends with hemstitching. Wet-finish in warm water, press flat while damp, and allow to dry. The alternating block texture becomes more pronounced after pressing.

items.problem: The block texture is not visible in the finished cloth
items.cause: The sett is too close, preventing the float blocks from showing
items.fix: Resample at a more open sett. The float length is only two threads, so the sett needs to be open enough for these short floats to stand clear of the ground.

items.problem: The selvedges draw in on one side but not the other
items.cause: Uneven tension on the warp or uneven beat applied across the width
items.fix: Check warp beam tension for even tension across all threads. Use a temple to hold the cloth to the correct width as you weave.
